explanation_md: Case 1 prints `A`, then `[[fallthrough]]` intentionally drops into case 2, which prints `B` and then breaks. Case 3 is never reached.

options.a: The `int` keyword is not allowed inside a switch body
options.b: A jump to `case 2` would bypass the initialization of `val`, leaving it uninitialized
options.c: `val` must be declared before the switch statement
options.d: Each case label defines a separate scope, so `val` would go out of scope at the next `case`

explanation_md: The entire switch body is one scope. Jumping to `case 2` skips the `int val = 42;` initializer, but `val` is still in scope and could be read uninitialized. C++ forbids such jumps across initializations. The fix is to wrap the case body in `{ }` to make `val` local to that block.
`n` is 3, so `case 3:` matches. It prints `three` and breaks. `case 2:` and `case 1:` are never reached. The `[[fallthrough]]` in case 2 only matters if case 2 is actually entered.

prompt_md: What is the purpose of the `[[fallthrough]]` attribute in C++17?

options.a: It forces the switch to restart from the first case
options.b: It documents intentional fallthrough and silences compiler warnings about missing `break`
options.c: It makes the case body execute even if the value does not match
options.d: It prevents any variable declarations inside that case body

explanation_md: `[[fallthrough]]` tells both the compiler and human readers that the absence of `break` is deliberate. Compilers that warn about implicit fallthrough (`-Wimplicit-fallthrough`) treat an annotated fallthrough as intentional and suppress the warning. It does not change runtime behavior — execution still falls through as it would without a `break`.
